The Fortress Within
nytimes_articles.abstract:
We Israelis woke up yesterday morning and saw the stuff of which our dreams are made -- for better and for worse. At the time of this writing, I still do not know the final election results, but I do know one thing: After three years  in which Yitzhak Rabin and Shimon Peres tried to lead Israel into a different future, into an era of peace, it is clear that while our heads may have surged forward, our heels are still dug deep into the ground. 

     At a certain moment in his life, Mr. Rabin looked two steps ahead and decided that Israel was strong enough to take a calculated risk. He shook Yasir Arafat's hand.

Books in Brief: NONFICTION;Popular Science
nytimes_articles.abstract: Henry Holt & Company, in its series "Scientific American Focus," has the rather interesting goal of taking areas of science that are likely to be in the  news or are generating controversy and making them clear to the average newspaper reader. The first three volumes, all written by science journalists and each costing $22.50, are written in simple, straightforward language and are embellished with numerous photographs and diagrams. MEDICATION OF THE MIND, by Scott Veggeberg, grapples with one of the many deep and difficult problems uncovered by our new understanding that life depends, at bottom, on chemistry. Specialized molecules in the brain, called receptors, seem to govern our moods and general feelings, and drugs like Prozac, which affect molecular chemistry, are the first fruits of this new understanding. But as more and more illnesses are dealt with by manipulating the brain's chemistry, traditional mental health  practitioners find themselves fighting a rear-guard action. COSMIC COLLISIONS, by Dana Desonie, takes on the bombardment of the earth by pieces of debris from  interplanetary space. Starting with the Big Bang and the formation of the solar  system, the book describes the historical process by which humans came to realize that large stones occasionally fall from the sky. My only problem was a  sense that Ms. Desonie was trying too hard to make the subject relevant, perhaps underestimating its inherent interest and her own ability to spin a good story.  THE LANGUAGE OF ANIMALS, by Stephen Hart, is full of interesting tidbits about the communication inherent in the feather displays of peacocks, the mechanisms for sound production in dolphins, and modern experiments on the language capabilities of chimpanzees. What is missing is a single coherent theme, or at least some direction as to which topics are sidelights and which bear on deep scientific questions. If these books are any indication of what is to come, I look forward to the next volumes with great anticipation.   James Trefil

BOOKEND;The Word and the Web
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Edward Mendelson is a professor of English at Columbia University and a contributing editor at PC Magazine. 

     When the Benedictine monks at the Monastery of Christ in the Desert, in New Mexico, created a Web site on the Internet, they claimed to be reviving a tradition that began when monastic scribes created the first illuminated manuscripts. One of the monks told a reporter for The New York Times that their  work "goes back to the ancient tradition of the scribes, taking information and  making it beautiful, into art." But the relation between modern Web sites and medieval scriptoria, or writing rooms, is even closer than these monks may have  guessed. The technology that connects all the millions of pages on the World Wide Web derives ultimately from techniques invented by the scribes and scholars who copied out the Bible more than a thousand years ago.

MUTUAL FUNDS;Investing Abroad With a Conscience: It's Not Easy
nytimes_articles.abstract:
JUST as Kathie Lee Gifford wept on her morning TV show after it became known  that a line of clothing sold under her name was made by Honduran children working for pennies a day, Americans who buy mutual funds that invest in foreign markets might be appalled if they knew where their money really was going. 

     Depending on the fund, the money could be financing companies that are cutting the rain forests, threatening a precious habitat in Chile or using child labor. And perusing a list of the fund's holdings would probably be of little help in determining which companies are at fault.

Punishing the Little Guy and Calling It Reform
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Bob Dole's departure from the Senate should ease the way for campaign finance reform, something that never made it onto his agenda when he was majority leader. That would be too bad. For despite the evident failings of the status quo, the leading plan now before Congress would do far more harm than good. 

     The measure -- endorsed by groups like Common Cause and sponsored in the Senate by Mr. Dole's friend John McCain of Arizona -- has a worthy goal: to curtail the influence of moneyed fixers. But it is fraught with provisions that  would cede more ground to the system's most entrenched and powerful players, trample the First Amendment rights of ordinary citizens and undermine efforts to encourage the broad-based participation that serves as a counterweight to the influence of political insiders.

nytimes_articles.headline: WEDDINGS;Jeffrey Zucker and Caryn Nathanson
In the Tiniest Places, Schools Big Cities Would Emulate
nytimes_articles.abstract:
PERCHED on a ridge of a long, narrow canyon, the Jamestown School sits like a sentry over this old mining town of 275 people, as it has for more than a century. It has beat back financial pressures, efforts to consolidate and educational theory that once said bigger is better. 

     Through it all, this little elementary school of two classrooms, 30 students  and two teachers has flourished, defying educational trends and demographic shifts. Students learn science using the mountains as their classroom. Math and  writing lessons are supplemented through a lab with five computers, which are also connected to the Internet. Best of all, parents say, is the school's student-teacher ratio.

nytimes_articles.abstract: According to a new poll conducted for the League of Women Voters, 57 percent  of nonvoters nationwide said they would rather work overtime than vote, 30 percent said they would rather go to a sale at a favorite store and 27 percent said they would sooner watch a new episode of a television program. The survey concluded that people stay clear of the polls not because they are any more alienated than those who vote, but because they simply prefer to do other things.   RICHARD L. BERKE

VIEWPOINT;Banks Must Gird for the Test Ahead
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AMERICAN banks have come back a long way from the financial distress of the early 1990's. In the near term, they should continue to benefit from steady economic growth, from relatively mild inflation and from an accommodating monetary policy by the Federal Reserve. 

     But within a year or two, this benign backdrop will be tested by questions about how the current expansion will end. When the economy turns down, many banks may discover some serious credit problems. Today, warning signs of future  credit disturbances, while still faint, are becoming more visible.

Smoking for the Thrill of It
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The number of high school students who smoke is on the rise, according to a recent report by the Federal Centers for Disease Control. Anti-smoking groups used this news to call for regulation of cigarettes by the Food and Drug Administration and to renew their attacks on a counterplan by Philip Morris to curb teen-age smoking. 

     It's a given that any restriction suggested by the tobacco industry is not intended to inhibit its sales. But what concerns me is the F.D.A.'s proposals to discourage teen-agers from smoking. The agency doesn't seem to understand the psychology of adolescents -- something that Philip Morris and other tobacco manufacturers seem to understand all too well.

TRAVEL ADVISORY;Drought Limits Access To Southwest Forests

Exploring a Red Rock World
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SNUGGLED in central Arizona's Verde Valley, between scorching deserts to the  south and high, snowy mountains to the north, Sedona's weather-sculptured, red sandstone buttes and spires attract three million tourists a year. Winters are mild, and the 4,500-foot altitude tempers summer heat. 

     Local residents boast that their Mogollon Ridge location, surrounded by huge  national and state forests, is second in natural wonder and majesty only to the  Grand Canyon of the Colorado River, two hours' drive to the north. A local saying has it, "God made the Grand Canyon but He lives in Sedona." And Hollywood has spread the gospel by showing the red rocks as a backdrop in more than 65 Western films.

Books in Brief: FICTION;Starting Over
nytimes_articles.abstract: Before he married Eva Wasierski, Tom Riley's life was "so uneventful, so without particulars, it might have been ordered from a catalogue." An energetic  and opinionated world traveler, Eva prodded and enlivened this quiet pet-shop owner who had rarely ventured outside his native Chicago. They had planned to go to Egypt to celebrate their 10th wedding anniversary -- but instead Tom finds himself alone, mourning Eva's death in Lago de Luz, the small Mexican town where they spent their honeymoon. "You can't run from sorrow," his sister warns  him. To which, with admirable logic, he replies, "I can cry in some warmer place, at least." As the opening chapters of Sandra Scofield's latest novel, A CHANCE TO SEE EGYPT (HarperCollins, $22), make plain, Tom's journey is a metaphor: without Eva, he thinks he cannot cover new ground. But when, shortly into his stay, an incident at the hotel leads him to take up residence elsewhere, we understand that Tom is ready to venture beyond his past. Drawn to  the strength of a local woman, he slowly awakens to his love for her beautiful and spirited 18-year-old daughter, aptly named Divina. Ambushed by his emotions, Tom is aware that "he had not so much earned his joy as accepted it." While this May-December romance may seem terribly familiar, Ms. Scofield (whose novel "Beyond Deserving" was a finalist for the National Book Award) gives it her own  skillful twist. With its lively cast of characters and its vividly rendered setting, "A Chance to See Egypt" emerges as an absorbing story that allows us to delight in Tom Riley's elation at the unexpected gift of a life renewed.   Janet Kaye

SOAPBOX;What a Waste
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN the good old days, New Jersey had a free-market garbage disposal system. It was a mess. 

     Garbage trucks clogged the roads around vast, unsightly, foul-smelling dumps. Valuable recyclable materials were entombed along with unsalvageable waste. As environmental concerns forced the closing of these so-called sanitary landfills, many cities and towns sent their garbage out of state, only to jockey frantically for new disposal sites when existing ones in other states were closed. And in the chaos, disposal costs skyrocketed -- along with property taxes to pay for the increase.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: DOWNTOWN;Wetlands Club Can Be Yours, for an Eco-Price
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Time may be running out for Wetlands, the TriBeCa nightclub that supports environmental causes and that had been a spawning ground for neo-hippie bands including the Spin Doctors, Blues Traveler, Dave Matthews Band and Joan Osborne. 

     Larry Bloch, the owner of the club at 161 Hudson Street, put it up for sale last year and had hoped to sell it by this summer -- but only to a buyer who would uphold his vision. Otherwise, he said, he would return the lease to the building's landlord. "The licensing agreement to use the name 'Wetlands,' " Mr.  Bloch said, "will only be offered to someone committed to continuing to fund our environmental organization, and to recycling, among other things."

nytimes_articles.abstract: Open Heart By A. B. Yehoshua. Translated by Dalya Bilu. 498 pp. New York: Doubleday. $24.95.    THE pre-eminence of A. B. Yehoshua in Israeli literature rests on a series of stories and novels that describe, with a very this-worldly psychological realism, the way Israelis live their daily lives within the immediate actualities of war and political turmoil. His most recent book, the extraordinary "Mr. Mani," was an exploration of five generations of a Sephardic  family.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: DOWNTOWN;Ludlow Street Cafe Changes Hands (Not to Mention Eras)
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Nine years ago Ludlow Street was a virtual no man's land. A sparsely populated residential area with lots of condemned tenements, it was void of night life for locals like Woody Fox, let alone for uptown trendsetters. 

     What was needed, Mr. Fox decided, was a bar. He and a silent partner started  the Ludlow Street Cafe, a cramped dive with live music including the house band Beat Rodeo, which has performed Monday nights for eight years. "We opened this place because I lived in the building and nobody wanted to come and visit me down here," Mr. Fox recalled. "I wanted to make this area a more desirable place to hang out."

THEATER;Tony Moments to Remember (and a Few to Forget)
nytimes_articles.abstract:
1947 Easter Sunday, April 6, 1947. Scene: The Waldorf-Astoria Hotel, where the first Tony Awards ceremony takes place. Amazingly, there is no best-musical  prize, although "Brigadoon," "Street Scene" and "Finian's Rainbow" had opened that season. Winners receive tasteful scrolls and a compact or cigarette lighter. 

     1948 Depending on the kindness of nearsighted voters, "Mister Roberts" beats  "A Streetcar Named Desire." (But which one has had more Broadway revivals?)

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: CITY ISLAND;Overdue at This Library? Space
nytimes_articles.abstract:
After a year of delays and budgeting problems caused by a clerical error, expansion has begun on the small, storefront library on City Island that holds the region's largest maritime collection. 

     Ground was broken last Tuesday by Arnold Haber, the owner of the property, who leases the space to the New York Public Library for $34,661 a year. Mr. Haber agreed to pay for the expansion, which will increase the library's space to 5,000 square feet, from 1,950 square feet. In exchange, the city agreed to pay $108,750 in annual rent for the next 20 years.

ON THE MAP;In Kearny, Celebrating the Benefits of a Day on the Marsh
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Several times a week, when the weather is fair, 83-year-old Leo Koncher pushes his canoe through the streets of Kearny. He's on his way to the freshwater marsh at the edge of town. 

     A retired machinist, Mr. Koncher moves his canoe on a homemade rig of bicycle tires. Down at the waterside he unstraps the wheels, puts them in the canoe and shoves off. He's out for a morning or maybe even a whole day of exploration  through the channels and lagoons of the marsh.

John Abbott, 90; Was English Actor
The Hill Towns Of Sicily: Above the Fray
nytimes_articles.abstract:
EVERY Sicilian hill town has a particular color to its stone, a texture to its bread, a tang to its wine and a twist to its dialect.  Built high for protection, they are the island's secret life, towering above the countryside in isolated splendor. Three of my favorites are Polizzi Generosa, Geraci Siculo and Sperlinga, in the Madonie and Nebrodi ranges near the heart of Sicily. 

     The last five miles of the road to Polizzi brings you to a dizzying height of 3,009 feet above sea level. The mountain commands a view of a valley planted with pear, almond and hazelnut trees. In March the blossoming orchards seem to fizz pink and white, and even in summer the morning air is chilled, effervescent and intoxicating.

We'll Always Have ... Questions
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE John Travolta character in "Pulp Fiction" had it right about Paris -- things there are the same as here, "just a little bit different." His interest was piqued by the McDonald's special (a quarter-pounder transformed by the metric system and nostalgia for the monarchy into The Royal). Having just returned from a month in France, I share his quotidian observations. Mine, however, are awash in puzzlement. Would a French person take pity on me and answer the following: 

      Do you really save money on those timers?
